EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front during the afternoon hours across central and east-central Wisconsin.  The strongest storms produced large hail and gusty winds as they tracked through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines in New Denmark. The time of this event is an estimate based on radar data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree and power pole in Maribel. The time of this event is an estimate based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of weakening showers and thunderstorms tracked through east-central Wisconsin during the late morning hours.  There was a microburst with one of the storms in Outagamie County, which downed some trees, power lines, and large branches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed small to medium trees, large branches, and power lines in Freedom. The time of this event is an estimate based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm moved across the Upper Midwest on January 17th and 18th. The storm generally dropped 3 to 6 inches of snow across the area followed by a light coating of ice from freezing drizzle. Despite the snow becoming ice crusted, wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph broke up the ice and snow, producing some blowing and drifting snow from the morning of the 18th through much of the afternoon. The highest reported snowfall amount was 8 inches near Owen (Clark County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 3 to 5 inches of snow fell across Buffalo County. A period of freezing drizzle created a coating of ice on top of the snow. Some blowing and drifting snow occurring during the morning and afternoon of the 18th with wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small, but intense, band of lake effect snow developed on the afternoon of the 11th at the far western end of Lake Superior near the Twin Ports. This band dropped over 6 inches of snowfall on the Superior area before slowly pivoting northward up the North Shore of Lakes Superior overnight. Snowfall totals were not as high along the North Shore, however.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system, which produced heavy snow during the onset, switched over to freezing drizzle, then back to light snow before transitioning to blowing snow and blizzard conditions.  The storm started Friday morning, January 17th with a surge of initial deep moisture advecting northward across the Upper Midwest. A dry layer at mid-levels kept the snowfall across southwest, and south central Minnesota before this layer saturated by noon. There was a 3 to 5 hour period of heavy snowfall that produced snowfall rates of 1 inch per hour. A few areas in southwest Minnesota had already received 4 to 6 inches of snow before noon. This area of snow moved northeast and caused heavy snow bands across east central and central Minnesota, as well as west central Wisconsin by the afternoon. As quickly as the snow started, it ended by the late afternoon, and early evening as a dry slot at mid-levels cutoff any ice crystal producing clouds. This dry slot, and the accompany freezing drizzle once the snow ended, caused a layer of ice to form on the snow that fell earlier. ||By sunrise, ice crystal producing clouds spread across the Upper Midwest as the main upper storm moved across the area. Periods of light snow fell across the region, but most of the accumulating snowfall had ended. The cold front moved across Minnesota, and into western Wisconsin by noon. Temperatures fell 10 to 20 degrees behind this front along with strong gusty winds developing. These winds created significant blowing and drifting snow in western Wisconsin, especially in open country.  The winds slowly abated during the evening, but blowing snow continued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall rates of 1/2 inch per hour occurred during the late afternoon and evening of Friday, January 17th. Gusty winds accompany the snowfall and continued through the storm. Some freezing drizzle also occurred. Locally 5 to 6 inches of snow fell on Friday, and early Saturday. Areas of blowing and drifting snow occurred on Saturday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person was killed in a single vehicle accident on State Highway 29 east of Owen (Clark County). The driver lost control, went into the median and was thrown from the vehicle. Light snow had been falling for most of the afternoon and the roads were reported to be slippery at the time of the accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person was killed in a single vehicle accident east of Owen on State Highway 29. The driver lost control, the vehicle went into the median and the driver was thrown from the vehicle. Light snow has been falling for most of the afternoon with the roads reported to be slippery at the time of the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a warm front over southwest Wisconsin during the early evening of March 28th. These storms spawned a tornado near Potosi (Grant County) that was on the ground for just over 6 miles before dissipating west of Ellenboro (Grant County). One house and numerous farm buildings were damaged along with trees and power lines along the path of the tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down north of Potosi and move northeast for 6 miles before dissipating west of Ellenboro. One house, numerous farm buildings, trees and power lines were damaged along the path of the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed over the Central Plains on the 28th and then moved northeast over southeastern Minnesota by the morning of the 29th. This spread snowfall across parts of the Northland and areas along and downwind of Lake Superior experienced lake and terrain enhancement of the snowfall. While areas immediately adjacent to Lake Superior along the South Shore and Twin Ports area did not receive heavy snowfall, areas inland above the terrain ridge saw between 6 and 8 inches of accumulation.
The WI River at Portage reached moderate flood stage due to melting snow and rainfall. It crested the early morning of April 1st at 18.18 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WI River at Portage reached moderate flood stage due to melting snow and rainfall. It crested the early morning of April 1st at 18.18 feet. Numerous roads are flooded in the Blackhawk Park and Long Lake areas across the river from Portage. Floodwaters surround seasonal homes along Old River Rd. Water is at the base of the  Caledonia/Lewiston Levee near Old River Rd. In the Town of Dekorra, water is over portions of River Oaks Rd. and Oak Knoll Dr., while about a dozen homes are flooded on Hwy V.  This flooding continued into early April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record high lake levels on Lake Michigan caused some isolated flooded areas near the lake on an ongoing basis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas around the Kewaunee Marina were often under water due to record high lake levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record high lake levels on Lake Michigan caused some isolated flooded areas near the lake on an ongoing basis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North Bayshore Road was often under water due to record high lake levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved across parts of western Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening of May 26th. These storms blew down trees and power lines near Owen (Clark County) with one of the trees landing on a house. Additional storms during the evening caused more wind damage with trees blown down in La Crosse and Barre Mills (La Crosse County). In Onalaska (La Crosse County), a power pole was blown down along with some trees. The storms also spawned a funnel cloud near Greenwood (Clark County).

In Onalaska (La Crosse County), a power pole was blown down along with some trees. The storms also spawned a funnel cloud near Greenwood (Clark County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down in and near Owen. One tree landed on a house and over 1,100 customers were without power after the storms moved through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of showers and storms moved across portions of western Wisconsin during the morning of May 28th. This activity produced rainfall totals of 2 to 4 inches that resulted in a couple of mud and rock slides west of Tomah (Monroe County) that closed a portion of State Highway 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches produced some isolated flash flooding west of Tomah. A couple of rock and mudslides closed a portion of State Highway 16 near Jackson Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak mid level shortwave created several rounds of strong to severe storms from the late morning through the evening hours across central and north-central Wisconsin.  The strongest storm produced large hail as it tracked through north-central Wisconsin in the late afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell in Summit Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of Tuesday, May 26th, a surface low was noted in southwest Iowa with a broad area of southeast flow along a surface boundary northward across southern Minnesota and into Wisconsin. Scattered thunderstorms started to develop in the warm sector by the mid afternoon across western Wisconsin and Iowa. These storms moved northward into an area of MLCAPEs of 1500-2000 J/kg with bulk shear values of around 30 knots. One storm moved northward across Dunn, and into Barron county and produced wind damage. Another storm produced a funnel cloud in Pepin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A social media picture of a funnel cloud.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down near Fountain City. One of the downed trees blocked both lanes of a road.

This activity produced rainfall totals of 2 to 4 inches that resulted in a couple of mud and rock slides west of Tomah (Monroe County) that closed a portion of State Highway 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rainfall report of 3.70 was received from the Melvina area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of Tuesday, May 26th, a surface low was noted in southwest Iowa with a broad area of southeast flow along a surface boundary northward across southern Minnesota and into Wisconsin. Scattered thunderstorms started to develop in the warm sector by the mid afternoon across western Wisconsin and Iowa. These storms moved northward into an area of MLCAPEs of 1500-2000 J/kg with bulk shear values of around 30 knots. One storm moved northward across Dunn, and into Barron county and produced wind damage. Another storm produced a funnel cloud in Pepin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was barn roof wind damage near Hillsdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small wildfire occurred May 6th east of Cottonville (Adams County) in the Town of Richfield. The burned remains of a person was found with the cause of death determined to be cardiac related because of extreme stress and exertion related to the fire. The fire consumed about 3 acres and was started by burning debris.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person died as a result of a small wildfire east of Cottonville. The cause of death was cardiac arrest related to extreme stress or exertion from the fire. The fire only burned about 3 acres and started as a result of burning debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds of 30 to 40 mph caused dust and dirt from a nearby farmers field to be lifted into the air, reducing visibilities to near zero across a small portion of Interstate 39 and Coundy D north of Plainfield.  There was a multiple car accident of 30 to 35 cars on north and southbound lanes of Interstate 39 and the interstate was closed through the early evening hours.  The cars were either severely damaged or totaled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, slow-moving low pressure system drifted southeast across the upper Midwest over a 3 day period from May 17 through May 19. This system interacted with copious moisture streaming north from the Gulf of Mexico that resulted in several rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall across Wisconsin, northern Illinois and Lower Michigan. Widespread rainfall amounts of 3 to 5 inches fell across much of east-central and northeast Wisconsin with a few locations breaking daily rainfall totals. The heavy rainfall was also accompanied by strong northeast winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph. The combination of heavy rainfall, record high Lake Michigan water levels and northeast winds, led to widespread flooding and beach erosion at shoreline areas along the Bay of Green Bay and Lake Michigan. The strong northeast winds hindered the drainage of water from the Fox and East Rivers into the mouth of the bay, which contributed to flooding in parts of downtown Green Bay and further inland along the East River. Significant flooding was also observed in the city of Oconto along the Oconto River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of record high lake levels on the Bay of Green Bay, 3 to 5 inches of rainfall, and strong northeast winds caused flooding along the shoreline in Brown County. Numerous streets were closed along the Bay of Green Bay due to the high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of Monday, May 25th, a single thunderstorm developed in Wabasha County and moved northward into Pepin County. This storm formed along a weak surface boundary across southeast Minnesota. Severe weather parameters were weak but the morning sounding at the MPX NWS indicated MUCAPEs of around 200-300 J/kg. Mid-level lapse rates were moist adiabatic of around 6.5 c/kg between 850-500 mb. Freezing levels were around 10,500 feet with bulk shear values of 30-35 kts. These parameters were enough to produce severe weather from the city of Pepin, north-northeast to Durand. This storm weakened as another storm developed in eastern Pepin County and moved northeast into Eau Claire and Chippewa Counties of west central Wisconsin. More severe hail reports were noted, but there was a brief downburst that caused damage in the northwest part of Eau Claire, Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A social media picture indicated 2 inch diameter hail fell in Pepin. Numerous spotters also reported hail as large as 2 inches in Pepin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rivers continued to flood into early May after several inches of rain fell in late April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fox River at New Munster reached minor flood stage, cresting at 12.46 feet on May 1st. Water is up to the first floor levels of some homes in the Town of Wheatland and the Village of Salem Lakes along Riverside Drive and Shorewood Drive. Floodwaters surround a home at the intersection of Highway 50 and Highway W in the Town of Wheatland area. Water is about 12 inches deep over 77th Street in the Town of Wheatland area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought several rounds of moderate to heavy rain across central and east-central Wisconsin during the 27th and 28th.  2 to 4 inches fell across the region during these two days, which caused street flooding and road closures across the area.  Most of the flood reports were concentrated in the Green Bay metro area, especially along the Fox and East rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of street flooding and road closures with water up to the curbs in the Green Bay metro area during the late morning and afternoon hours.  Many of the reports were along the Fox and East rivers.  There was a report of a stranded vehicle and a rescue of an elderly female from the vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure area brought a 18-24 hour period of moderate to sometimes heavy rainfall. 3 to 6 inches of rain fell over far eastern WI, which resulted in river, creek, and lowland flooding. Numerous roads were flooded and closed. A small number of water rescues were executed. One indirect death occurred when a retaining wall collapsed onto an elderly woman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall and persistent northeast winds causing high waves resulted in significant lakeshore bluff erosion in parts of Ozaukee County, along Lake Michigan.  In the Town of Grafton, an estimated 4 feet of bluff eroded causing the foundation of one home on Lake Shore Road and Pioneer Road to be exposed.  A significant mud slide occurred at Lion's Den Gorge State Park that did necessitate the rescue of two hikers that were trying to retrieve a dog.  The hikers had only minor injuries.  There was also a hill slide at the Port Washington High School/Community Pool.  This resulted in a compromised roadway.  Small sections of the bluff have failed up and down the Ozaukee county shoreline, including Upper Lake Park in Port Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly flow led to warm, moist, and unstable conditions across southern WI. Scattered thunderstorms developed with a few of those producing damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four horse shelters collapsed by downburst winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through northwest Wisconsin the night of the 26-27 and dropped a widespread area of 2-3 inches of rain over parts of Sawyer County. While most areas did not see any issues, a privately owned dam on the Mosquito Brook Flowage failed and led to minor flash flooding downstream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall the night before of 2-3 inches led to the failure of a privately-owned dam on the Mosquito Brook Flowage. When the dam gave way, a surge of water moved downstream about 2.5 miles to the Namekagon River. Culverts at both Mosquito Brook Road and Porky's Road were overwhelmed with two feet of water about 100 yards wide at both locations. This flooding cut off access to multiple homes and caused damage to roadways. Additionally, bridge damage was reported to an ATV trail and to the Birkebeiner ski trail at the crossings of Mosquito Brook. No damage was reported to any buildings or homes. Roads remained closed into the 28th before water receded.

The driver did exit the scene safely.  The Root River also flooded and closed both the north and southbound lanes of Wisconsin 241 over far southeast Franklin the morning of May 18th. The road remained closed into the afternoon.  A portion of Oakwood Road is flooded and closed. There is widespread flooding to agricultural land along the basin.

A strong late season storm system brought significant snow, sleet and gusty winds to northeast Wisconsin from the late morning of April 12 through the early morning hours of April 13.  The wet, heavy snow and strong winds produced some isolated power outages across north-central Wisconsin. A heavy band of snow from 6 to 15 inches fell north of a Marshfield to Wausaukee line with lighter amounts to the south.||Some of the higher snowfall totals included 15.0 inches in Spread Eagle (Florence Co.), 13.0 inches in Gleason (Lincoln Co.), 12.8 inches in Pelican Lake (Oneida Co.), 12.5 inches near Merrill (Lincoln Co.) as well as Argonne (Forest Co.), 11.8 inches near Amberg (Marinette Co.), and 10.0 inches in Lac Vieux Desert (Vilas Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall total in Portage County was 5.2 inches in Rosholt. Sleet with icing amounts less than one tenth of an inch fell before changing over to all snow. Although snow totals were not as high as many locations, gusty north to northwest winds caused blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple lines of high-based showers and isolated thunderstorms led to severe wind gusts that knocked down and uprooted trees. This resulted in downed power lines and several thousand customers without power over southeast WI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight-line winds uprooting a large pine tree and shearing off another large pine tree. Pine tree branches also knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several thunderstorms, some severe, developed across northeast South Dakota during the late evening of Monday, April 6th. These storms moved across west-central, central and east-central Minnesota during the overnight hours, then moved into west-central Wisconsin and reintensified near Spooner. The storm moved east-southeast into northern Rusk County. Videos and pictures of large hail were noted from Ladysmith, east to Hawkins, Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a swath of large hail, the largest was hen size northeast of Ladysmith that occurred from north of Bruce, east-southeast to Hawkins. Radar data, which kept the intensity throughout the storm that moved across Rusk County was used for timing and length.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Western Wisconsin started April with some river flooding along portions of the Yellow and Mississippi Rivers. The flooding was the result of runoff from snow that melted in late March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from melted snow pushed the Mississippi River out of its banks in La Crosse. The river crested over a half foot above the flood stage at 12.67 feet on April 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Western Wisconsin started April with some river flooding along portions of the Yellow and Mississippi Rivers. The flooding was the result of runoff from snow that melted in late March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from melted snow pushed the Yellow River out of its banks in Necedah. The river crested around a half foot above the flood stage at 15.62 feet on April 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The WI River at Portage reached moderate flood stage after runoff from rain and snow melt.  It crested the early morning of April 1st at 18.18 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WI River at Portage reached moderate flood stage due to melting snow and rainfall. It crested the early morning of April 1st at 18.18 feet. Numerous roads are flooded in the Blackhawk Park and Long Lake areas across the river from Portage. Floodwaters surround seasonal homes along Old River Rd. Water is at the base of the Caledonia/Lewiston Levee near Old River Rd. In the Town of Dekorra, water is over portions of River Oaks Rd. and Oak Knoll Dr., while about a dozen homes are flooded on Hwy V.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Monday, April 20th, a line of showers developed along a cold front over east-central Minnesota, and west-central Wisconsin. Very weak instability (200 J/KG) along the front limited any strong updrafts and therefore, there were very few lightning strikes within this band. Strong lapse rates during the afternoon allowed for good mixing and these convective showers were able to tap into the strong winds around 5000' above the surface. These winds were in the 40 to the 50-knot range and were able to mix down to the surface and create the severe wind gusts. ||There were numerous public and private weather stations that measured wind speeds between 40 and 55 mph within the convection. A couple of damaging wind reports occurred and a couple of reports of severe wind gusts were noted in the late afternoon in west central Wisconsin. There were also several wind reports of 50 to 56 mph measured around the Twin Cities metro area with this same line. ||Some of the higher wind speeds measured that were under the 58 mph include: |Hudson, Wisconsin measured 57 mph. |New Richmond, Wisconsin airport (AWOS) measured 56 mph. |St. Paul, Minnesota airport (ASOS) measured 56 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small shed was destroyed, a few shingles were blown off a roof near Boardman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure area brought several inches of rain to far eastern WI over a 24-40 hour period.  River and lake flooding occurred along with some road flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixty-Seventh Dr. was closed between 58th Rd. and Mill Ave. due to high water on a portion of the road.

Late Friday night, and into Saturday, April 12th, a strong system started organizing across the northern and central Rockies, with moisture building in the central plains ahead of the system.  A cold front moved as far south as southeast Minnesota, and west central Wisconsin before stalling Saturday night. A surface low developed across Colorado Saturday, before intensifying and moving east-northeast across the plains, and into the Great Lakes by Sunday night. Most of central and southern Minnesota, as well as west central Wisconsin, were in the cold side of this storm system. Only the far southeast part had enough warm air to cause some mixing of sleet, and rain before changing over to snow Sunday. This was noted with the next day visible satellite image which had a sharp cutoff of where no snow accumulated in the far southeast part of Minnesota. ||Due to the initial weak short waves ahead of the main storm system moving out into the plains Sunday morning, some light snow began in portions of southwest and south central Minnesota. Most of the snowfall prior to sunrise Sunday was light with light accumulations. Once stronger forcing develop Sunday morning, does the snowfall intensity increase. The initial band of heavier snowfall was noted in south central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin where 3 to 6 inches fell before early Sunday afternoon. By the afternoon, a secondary band of heavier snowfall began in southwest Minnesota, and slowly moved eastward during the afternoon, and into the early evening across west central Wisconsin before weakening. This band of heavier snowfall was related to strong forcing in the 850-700 MB layer. Snowfall amounts of 6 inches or more occurred in this band by the mid afternoon before  it tapered off and moved east into Wisconsin. This is when the bulk of the snow accumulated but lighter snowfall still remained over south central, east central Minnesota, as well as west central Wisconsin before ending early Monday morning.  ||The following are some of the higher snowfall totals:||11.8 inches near Ladysmith.|10.5 inches near Eau Claire.|9 inches near Boyceville. |8.5 inches near River Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southeast part of Barron County had the most snow with locally 6 to 9 inches. A report of 9.5 inches occurred near Chetek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure area brought several inches of rain to far eastern WI over a 24-40 hour period.  River and lake flooding occurred along with some road flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of Swift Lake, Lake Lorraine, Lake Wandawega, and the village of Walworth flooded after 2-3 inches of rain and resultant runoff into already high lake levels from last autumn. This flooded a small number of homes and other structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure area brought several inches of rain to far eastern WI over a 24-40 hour period.  River and lake flooding occurred along with some road flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Almost all of North Beach in Racine was underwater due to lakeshore flooding and record high water levels on Lake Michigan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracked along the WI and IL border and then northeast across lower MI. North to northwest winds gusted up to 45 mph with scattered downed tree limbs.  Saturated soils likely contributed to the downing of a few large trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were blown down in New Berlin due to gusty winds and saturated soils. Another large tree was blown down in Brookfield.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Root River Canal at Raymond reached minor flood stage, cresting at 10.57 feet on April 30th. This flooding continued into May 1st. Floodwaters cover a baseball diamond on 6 Mile Road. There is extensive flooding to lowland and agricultural land in the Town of Raymond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late season storm system brought significant snow, sleet and gusty winds to northeast Wisconsin from the late morning of April 12 through the early morning hours of April 13.  The wet, heavy snow and strong winds produced some isolated power outages across north-central Wisconsin. A heavy band of snow from 6 to 15 inches fell north of a Marshfield to Wausaukee line with lighter amounts to the south.||Some of the higher snowfall totals included 15.0 inches in Spread Eagle (Florence Co.), 13.0 inches in Gleason (Lincoln Co.), 12.8 inches in Pelican Lake (Oneida Co.), 12.5 inches near Merrill (Lincoln Co.) as well as Argonne (Forest Co.), 11.8 inches near Amberg (Marinette Co.), and 10.0 inches in Lac Vieux Desert (Vilas Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell on the 12th and 13th. Sleet with icing amounts less than one tenth of an inch fell before changing over to all snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record high lake levels on Lake Michigan caused some isolated flooded areas near the lake on an ongoing basis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North Bayshore Road was often under water due to record high lake levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record high lake levels on Lake Michigan caused some isolated flooded areas near the lake on an ongoing basis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas around the Kewaunee Marina were often under water due to record high lake levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elderly man died from burns sustained trying to extinguish a grass fire near Ettrick (Trempealeau County). The fire occurred during the morning of April 20th and was started by a burning pile of garbage. The automated weather observing equipment at the La Crosse (La Crosse County) airport indicated the relative humidity was around 35% with a southwest wind gusting to near 20 mph at the time of the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An elderly Ettrick man died after sustaining burns trying to put out a grassfire. The fire started as a result of a burning pile of garbage. The automated weather observing equipment at the La Crosse airport indicated the relative humidity was around 35% with a a southwest wind gusting to around 20 mph at the time of the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person was killed in a two vehicle accident near Potosi (Grant County) on the morning of April 8th. The driver of a pickup failed to stop for a stop sign at an intersection on U.S. Highway 61, collided with a semi-truck, and was thrown from the vehicle. According to the accident report, the incident occurred during dense fog. The automated weather observing equipment at the Platteville (Grant County) and Dubuque (Dubuque County) airports were both reporting dense fog with a quarter mile or less visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The driver of a pickup failed to stop for a stop sign at an intersection along U.S. Highway 61 near Potosi, collided with a semi-truck and was thrown from the vehicle. According to the accident report, the driver was pronounced dead at the scene of the accident and that dense fog was a contributing factor. The automated weather observing equipment at the Platteville and Dubuque airports were both reporting dense fog with a visibility of a quarter mile or less at the time of the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms moved across southwest Wisconsin during the evening of June 26th. These storms produced some damaging winds as they moved across Grant County. Trees and power lines were blown down near Glen Haven, Bloomington and Platteville. A shed was also damaged near Platteville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down near Glen Haven.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms moved across southwest Wisconsin during the evening of June 26th. These storms produced some damaging winds as they moved across Grant County. Trees and power lines were blown down near Glen Haven, Bloomington and Platteville. A shed was also damaged near Platteville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down near Bloomington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved across portions of north-central Wisconsin during the morning of April 7th. Some of these storms produced large hail with half dollar sized hail reported in Westboro (Taylor County). More storms developed during the late afternoon over southwest Wisconsin. These storms dropped quarter sized hail in Dickeyville (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell north of Westboro.

These storms dropped quarter sized hail in Dickeyville (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to quarter sized hail was reported in Dickeyville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure area brought several inches of rain to far eastern WI over a 24-40 hour period.  River and lake flooding occurred along with some road flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fox River at New Munster reached minor flood stage, cresting at 12.46 feet on May 1st. This flooding continued into the early evening of May 5th. Water is up to the first floor levels of some homes in the Town of Wheatland and the Village of Salem Lakes along Riverside Drive and Shorewood Drive. Floodwaters surround a home at the intersection of Highway 50 and Highway W in the Town of Wheatland area. Water is about 12 inches deep over 77th Street in the Town of Wheatland area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms moved across southwest Wisconsin during the evening of June 26th. These storms produced some damaging winds as they moved across Grant County. Trees and power lines were blown down near Glen Haven, Bloomington and Platteville. A shed was also damaged near Platteville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof was blown off a shed northeast of Platteville. Some tree limbs were also blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed over the Texas/Oklahoma Panhandle region on the 12th and then lifted northeast to the Straits of Mackinac and deepened considerably by the morning of the 13th. This low brought a narrow area of heavy snow to parts of northwest Wisconsin mainly during the afternoon of the 12th and into the overnight hours of the 13th, leaving a swath of 6 to 16 inches of snow from Washburn County eastward into Iron and Price counties. Snowfall amounts dropped off quickly to the south and the highest amounts were closer to Lake Superior where winds off the lake brought additional moisture into the system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late season storm system brought significant snow, sleet and gusty winds to northeast Wisconsin from the late morning of April 12 through the early morning hours of April 13.  The wet, heavy snow and strong winds produced some isolated power outages across north-central Wisconsin. A heavy band of snow from 6 to 15 inches fell north of a Marshfield to Wausaukee line with lighter amounts to the south.||Some of the higher snowfall totals included 15.0 inches in Spread Eagle (Florence Co.), 13.0 inches in Gleason (Lincoln Co.), 12.8 inches in Pelican Lake (Oneida Co.), 12.5 inches near Merrill (Lincoln Co.) as well as Argonne (Forest Co.), 11.8 inches near Amberg (Marinette Co.), and 10.0 inches in Lac Vieux Desert (Vilas Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across Vilas County included 10.0 inches in Lac Vieux Desert, 9.4 inches near Land O' Lakes, 9.2 inches in Presque Isle, and  8.7 inches in Eagle River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from Tropical Depression Cristobal fell across western Wisconsin on June 9th. This heavy rain resulted in some flash flooding across portions of Buffalo, Trempealeau and Taylor Counties. In Fountain City (Buffalo County), water entered into a home when the levels reached as high as a door. High water covered some city streets in Arcadia (Trempealeau County) and up to a foot of water covered roads in and near Gilman (Taylor County). The highest reported rainfall totals were 3.87 inches in Cochrane (Buffalo County) and 3.66 inches southeast of Mondovi (Buffalo County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from Tropical Depression Cristobal caused some flash flooding in Fountain City. Water entered a home when it reached the level of a door.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of freezing drizzle developed across portions of western Wisconsin during the morning of January 9th. Very icy conditions developed across La Crosse and Monroe Counties as a result of the freezing drizzle. Interstate 90 was closed in both directions near Tomah (Monroe County) after numerous accidents occurred in a six mile stretch.  The westbound lanes of Interstate 90 were also shutdown near Sparta (Monroe County). The eastbound lanes of Interstate 94 were closed north of Tomah after a semi crash. A total of 90 accidents were reported across Monroe County. In La Crosse County, a total of 83 crashes occurred across the county with another 34 in the city of La Crosse.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle created very icy conditions across Monroe County. A total of 90 accidents were reported with stretches of Interstate 90 shutdown near Tomah and Sparta. The eastbound lanes of Interstate 94 were closed north of Tomah with the westbound lanes reduced to one lane of traffic near Oakdale.

A significant late season storm system dropped heavy snow on portions of western Wisconsin on Easter Sunday, April 12th. Snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 11 inches and the highest reported total was 11 inches near Owen (Clark County). As the heavy snow fell, roads quickly became snow covered and the treacherous travel conditions continued into the evening as strong northwest winds created blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 5 to 9 inches of snow fell across Buffalo County. The highest reported total was 9 inches near Montana. Strong northwest winds created blowing and drifting snow during the late afternoon and evening hours.

The slow passage of a cold front resulted in mixed precipitation for much of southern WI. The precipitation was mainly freezing rain and some light snow. Accumulated ice from 0.10-0.25 inches was observed, while 1-3 inches of snow fell from far southwest WI to east central WI. Some vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred due to the freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations of 0.05-0.15 inches and snowfall of 1-2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Ice Jam and high river stages produced flooding in the city of Oconto, called the worst flooding in decades. Water surrounded many homes and some businesses and the city of Oconto declared a flood emergency.  Oconto had similar flooding from ice jams in the late 1800s, 1922, and 1986. An estimated $480,000 worth of damage was reported during this event. ||The flooding began during the previous month, the night of December 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam and high river stages produced flooding in the city of Oconto.  The high water mainly affected Pecor, McDonald, Water, Turner, Ontario, Bridge, First and Second streets.  The mobile home park on Farnsworth Avenue also Flooded. Washington Street by the Oconto County Courthouse also flooded as storm sewers overflowed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across Waupaca County included 8.2 inches in Clintonville and 8.0 inches near Iola.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 4 to 7 inches of snow fell across Trempealeau County. The highest reported total was 7 inches in Arcadia and near Independence. Strong northwest winds created blowing and drifting snow during the late afternoon and evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The slow passage of a cold front resulted in mixed precipitation for much of southern WI. The precipitation was mainly freezing rain and some light snow. Accumulated ice from 0.10-0.25 inches was observed, while 1-3 inches of snow fell from far southwest WI to east central WI. Some vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred due to the freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations of 0.10-0.20 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure passing by to the southeast resulted in an extended period of strong north to northeast winds along the Lake Michigan shoreline of southeast and east central Wisconsin.  Wind gusts at Milwaukee Mitchell Field gusted 40 to 50 knots at time from 9 pm CST the evening of January 10 through 3 pm CST the afternoon of January 11.  The persistent strong onshore winds combined with near record high Lake Michigan water levels produce severe lakeshore flooding and erosion at the Port of Milwaukee as well as at spots all along Lake Michigan from Kenosha to Sheboygan.  Wave heights were estimated to be in the 10 to 18 foot range at the height of the event the morning of January 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters due to high waves estimated 6 to 8 feet and near record Lake Michigan water levels spread across the Port of Milwaukee, causing the Port to close late in the morning on January 11th.  Carferry Drive and Lincoln Memorial Drive were closed leading into the Port of Milwaukee.  About 60 to 70 percent of Jones Island where Port of Milwaukee is located was flooded.  The flood water stripped dock wall material and covered railroad tracks with a foot of ice on Jones Island.  The Port of Milwaukee reopened 48 hours later on Monday, January 13th.  Flood erosion damage took place at other locations along the Milwaukee County shore: ||- South Shore Yacht Club: Damage to piers and docks.  Damage to break wall located just outside the club. |- Partial washout of Oak Leaf walking trail along South Shore Drive.  Also damage to trail near art museum.|- Riverfront boat launch damage.|- Veterans Park: Concrete washout of lakeshore.|- McKinley Marina gas dock collapse. Also integrity of parking lot and boat launch compromised.|- Warnimont Golf Course - 20' x 30' section of bluff collapse. ||Also section of North Point Light parking lot collapse and washout into lake. Also, 50' x 50' section of bluff collapse at Bay View and Lake Parks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record high lake levels on Lake Michigan caused some isolated flooded areas near the lake on an ongoing basis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North Bayshore Road was often under water due to high lake levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across north-central Wisconsin during the late afternoon of April 20th. The strong winds from these storms blew down trees and power lines across the northern sections of Clark County and in the Neillsville area (Clark County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down across the northern portions of Clark County as a line of thunderstorms moved across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire in the Necedah Wildlife Refuge (Juneau County) burned 240 acres of lowland marsh regions on April 18th. While there were no injuries because of the fire, some residents near the refuge had to be evacuated for safety precautions and State Highway 80 north of Necedah was closed. The remote automated weather station on the refuge reported favorable fire conditions as the relative humidity fell to under 25% with southwest winds of 15 to 20 mph gusting as high as 39 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire burned around 240 acres of lowland and marsh region in the Necedah Wildlife Refuge on April 18th. Some residents living near the refuge had to be evacuated for safety precautions and State Highway 80 was closed north of Necedah. The remote automated weather station located on the refuge indicated the relative humidity fell to under 25% during the afternoon with southwest winds of 15 to 20 mph gusting up to 39 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure passing by to the southeast resulted in an extended period of strong north to northeast winds along the Lake Michigan shoreline of southeast and east central Wisconsin.  Wind gusts at Milwaukee Mitchell Field gusted 40 to 50 knots at time from 9 pm CST the evening of January 10 through 3 pm CST the afternoon of January 11.  The persistent strong onshore winds combined with near record high Lake Michigan water levels produce severe lakeshore flooding and erosion at the Port of Milwaukee as well as at spots all along Lake Michigan from Kenosha to Sheboygan.  Wave heights were estimated to be in the 10 to 18 foot range at the height of the event the morning of January 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near record high Lake Michigan water levels along with strong winds and resultant high waves peaking at 10 to 15 feet caused considerable erosion and lakeshore flood damage along the Kenosha County lakeshore.  ||Some of the damage in the county included: ||Kemper Center County Park in Kenosha was severely damaged along the Lake Michigan shoreline due to high winds and waves.   One thousand, five hundred feet of shoreline was damaged.  Severe destruction occurred where voids in the shoreline were created, causing displaced rock and earth material.  ||The Kenosha shoreline between 71st Street and 76th Street was damaged.  A sinkhole developed and caused the shoreline to collapse in two locations.  Damage also occurred on the Kenosha Harbor walls and promenade due to high waves of at least 10 feet.  Lakeshore flooding also closed Kenosha streets including First Avenue, 50th Street, Fourth Avenue and 45th street.||The Village of Pleasant Prairie sustained lakeshore damage on Lake Shore Drive between 107th St and 113th Street, to about 1300 feet of shoreline.  A stream outlet just north of 110th St. was buried by lakeshore flooding and erosion, which resulted in flooding on upstream properties A 15-inch corrugated metal culvert end section required emergency excavation to alleviate the flooding.  Damage was also done at the South Beach area, Chiwaukee Beach and Prairie Shores Beach.  ||Lakeshore erosion also caused a Pleasant Prairie home to teeter on the edge of bluff overlooking Lake Michigan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracking through the southern Great Lakes caused gusty northeast winds across east-central and northeast Wisconsin, which caused some isolated damage and widespread lakeshore flooding in Brown and Oconto counties.  The lakeshore flooding damaged several homes near the bay of Green Bay, with over $500,000 of damage in northern Brown and southern Oconto counties. The northeast winds funneled down the bay of Green Bay, which backed up the Fox and East Rivers, causing flooding further inland in Green Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of record high lake levels on the Bay of Green Bay and strong northeast winds caused flooding along the shoreline in Door County. The high water caused road closures on portions of Memorial Drive and North Stevenson Pier Road between County Highway C and Woodlane.

The northeast winds funneled down the bay of Green Bay, which backed up the Fox and East Rivers, causing flooding further inland in Green Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds caused downed trees, power lines, and power outages in Manitowoc, Two Rivers, and Two Creeks.

The northeast winds funneled down the bay of Green Bay, which backed up the Fox and East Rivers, causing flooding further inland in Green Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of record high lake levels on the Bay of Green Bay and strong northeast winds backed up the waters on the Fox and East Rivers in Green Bay, causing flooding in the city of Green Bay.  Several streets were closed due to flooding along the Fox and East River in Green Bay.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines in Waupaca. The time of this event is an estimate based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracking through the southern Great Lakes caused gusty northeast winds across east-central and northeast Wisconsin, which caused some isolated damage and widespread lakeshore flooding in Brown and Oconto counties.  The lakeshore flooding damaged several homes near the bay of Green Bay, with over $500,000 of damage in northern Brown and southern Oconto counties. The northeast winds funneled down the bay of Green Bay, which backed up the Fox and East Rivers, causing flooding further inland in Green Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of record high lake levels on the Bay of Green Bay and strong northeast winds caused flooding along the shoreline in Brown County. The high water ranged from several inches to a few feet deep that approached or surrounded several residences and buildings along East Shore Drive, East Shore Circle, Esther Lane, Anapaula Lane, the boat launch by Bayshore Road, and the Coast Guard Station in Green Bay. One residence on East Shore Drive suffered significant damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective vortex moved across parts of the Northland during the early morning hours on June 30th. This feature, along with a moisture-rich low level jet, led to thunderstorms that were very efficient rain producers. Rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches in a fairly short amount of time led to some minor flooding issues in parts of Burnett County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall led to minor street flooding in front of the Burnett County Court House.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Cristobal moved through Wisconsin on June 9th into June 10th and spread heavy rainfall across parts of the state. Most of the Northland saw minimal effects from the system, but heavy rainfall was observed across Price County the night of the 9th. Measured rainfall of 2 to 4 inches was observed with isolated higher amounts in excess of 5 inches led to a few washed out roads and damaged culverts in the southeastern part of Price County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2 to 4 inches with localized amounts in excess of 5 inches fell over much of Price County. The heavier amounts led to flash flooding in the Town of Knox in the form of washed out roads and shoulders along with damaged culverts. This led to a disaster declaration for the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of record high lake levels on the Bay of Green Bay and strong northeast winds caused flooding along the shoreline in Oconto County. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 1-3 inches and snow covered roads. The snow began in the early evening of February 12th for portions of southern WI with relatively light winds at the time. However, the passage of an arctic front from the late evening into the early morning hours made for gusty north winds of 25 to 35 mph, blowing and drifting snow, reduced visibility, and continued hazardous travel. Some vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred from the evening into the next morning. A two-vehicle accident closed all southbound lanes of I/39/90/94 just north of highway 60 in southern Columbia County for approximately 2 hours during the early morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 2-3 inches. Gusty north winds of 25 to 35 mph with some blowing and drifting snow and reduced visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and front brought a prolonged period of snow to portions of southern WI. The snowfall was moderate to heavy at times, including during the evening commute. Many vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 3-6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record high lake levels on Lake Michigan caused some isolated flooded areas near the lake on an ongoing basis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas around the Kewaunee Marina were often under water due to record high lake levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record high lake levels on Lake Michigan caused some isolated flooded areas near the lake on an ongoing basis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North Bayshore Road was often under water due to record high lake levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving, compact low pressure system tracking through southern Wisconsin brought 6 to 10 inches of snow to central and northeast Wisconsin. Visibilities dropped down to a quarter of a mile or less for several hours as the heavy band of snow moved across the area. A two vehicle crash in the Town of Liberty in Outagamie County resulted in one fatality and one injury.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 10.3 inches in Polonia (Portage Co.), 10.0 inches in New Franken (Brown Co.) as well as Forestville (Door Co.), 9.8 inches in Wisconsin Rapids (Wood Co.), and 9.7 inches in Clintonville (Waupaca Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to ten inches of snow fell across Wood County. The highest snowfall totals included: 2 miles west of Wisconsin Rapids 9.8 inches, Wisconsin Rapids 8.5 inches and  Pittsville and Nekoosa 8.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed across the northern Rockies on Saturday, February 8th. This area of low pressure moved and weakened across South Dakota, and over northern Iowa by Sunday morning. However, a 120 knot jet across the Plains, combined with strong forcing at mid-levels of the atmosphere, generated a band of moderate to heavy snowfall across southern Minnesota starting early Sunday morning. This band of snow quickly moved across the southern part of the state by noon, and exited west central Wisconsin by the early afternoon. Snowfall rates as high as 1-2 inches per hour occurred with the most intense bands of snow. Lightning was also detected near Owatonna MN during the height of the storm. There was even an elevated instability layer along the nose of the intensifying low level jet that set up Sunday morning.||By noon Sunday, there was a band of 6 inches or greater of snowfall that fell across southern MN (including the southern part of the Twin Cities Metro area), then to Eau Claire, Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observers that measure snowfall across St. Croix County, reported 5 to 7 inches of snow before it tapered off early Sunday afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of relatively warm and moist air aloft and an upper level disturbance and front, brought several inches of snow to southern WI. Some areas received 6-10 inches.  Snow covered roads and low visibilities resulted in many vehicle slide-offs and accidents.  A large vehicle backup occurred on Brookfield Hill in Brookfield. A two-vehicle crash in rural Racine County resulted in one death and nine injured, when one vehicle lost control on snow covered roads and crossed the center line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 6-10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed across the northern Rockies on Saturday, February 8th. This area of low pressure moved and weakened across South Dakota, and over northern Iowa by Sunday morning. However, a 120 knot jet across the Plains, combined with strong forcing at mid-levels of the atmosphere, generated a band of moderate to heavy snowfall across southern Minnesota starting early Sunday morning. This band of snow quickly moved across the southern part of the state by noon, and exited west central Wisconsin by the early afternoon. Snowfall rates as high as 1-2 inches per hour occurred with the most intense bands of snow. Lightning was also detected near Owatonna MN during the height of the storm. There was even an elevated instability layer along the nose of the intensifying low level jet that set up Sunday morning.||By noon Sunday, there was a band of 6 inches or greater of snowfall that fell across southern MN (including the southern part of the Twin Cities Metro area), then to Eau Claire, Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observers that measure snowfall across Eau Claire County, reported 5 to 7 inches of snow before it tapered off Sunday afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 65 year old man was found dead in the snow in Glendale. The Milwaukee County Medical Examiner determined the cause of death as hypothermia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 72 year-old man died after being found lying in his driveway. The Milwaukee County Medical Examiner determined the probable cause of death as hypothermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 72 year-old man was found conscious and crawling on his driveway in the morning. He was taken to the hospital where he died. The Milwaukee County Medical Examiner determined the probable cause of death as hypothermia. He had a core body temperature of 79 degrees Fahrenheit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracking through southern Wisconsin brought accumulating snow to the region during the afternoon of February 17th through the morning hours of February 18th. The snow started moving into east-central Wisconsin during the afternoon of February 17th, before proceeding to spread across the entire region during the evening and overnight hours. The heaviest snow fell in a corridor from Wautoma to Door County during the early morning hours of February 18th with the highest totals over far northeast Wisconsin.  Most of the area received 4 to 8 inches, with lower amounts northwest and southeast of this corridor.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 8.3 inches in Marinette (Marinette Co.), 7.5 inches in Stiles (Oconto Co.), and 7.0 inches near Sister Bay (Door Co.) as well as near Ephraim (Door Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across southern Marinette County included 8.3 inches in Marinette and 6.5 inches near Peshtigo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved rather quickly across the region on February 9th. This storm produced a period of heavy snow with rates of 1 to 2 inches an hour at times. Accumulations across portions of western Wisconsin were generally in the 4 to 10 inch range. The highest reported amounts were 10.5 inches in Union Center (Juneau County) and 10 inches near Black River Falls (Jackson County), in New Lisbon (Juneau County), in Barre Mills (La Crosse County) and near Tomah (Monroe County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 3 to 7 inches of snow across Crawford County. The highest reported total was 7 inches near De Soto.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 1-3 inches and snow covered roads. The snow began in the early evening of February 12th for portions of southern WI with relatively light winds at the time. However, the passage of an arctic front from the late evening into the early morning hours made for gusty north winds of 25 to 35 mph, blowing and drifting snow, reduced visibility, and continued hazardous travel. Some vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred from the evening into the next morning. A two-vehicle accident closed all southbound lanes of I/39/90/94 just north of highway 60 in southern Columbia County for approximately 2 hours during the early morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 1-2 inches. Gusty north winds of 25 to 35 mph with some blowing and drifting snow and reduced visibility. A two-vehicle accident closed all southbound lanes of I/39/90/94 just north of highway 60 in southern Columbia County for approximately 2 hours during the early morning.

Accumulations across portions of western Wisconsin were generally in the 4 to 10 inch range. The highest reported amounts were 10.5 inches in Union Center (Juneau County) and 10 inches near Black River Falls (Jackson County), in New Lisbon (Juneau County), in Barre Mills (La Crosse County) and near Tomah (Monroe County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 5 to 6 inches of snow across Clark County. The highest reported total was 6.2 inches near Dorchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of heat and humidity triggered scattered thunderstorms across northern Wisconsin. Isolated wind damage was reported in Rhinelander.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees in the city of Rhinelander.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms produced gusty winds as they tracked through northern Wisconsin during the morning hours along a warm front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees, traffic lights, and a bank sign in Weston. The time of this report is an estimate based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system brought multiple rounds of severe storms to the Northland starting during the early morning hours of the 8th. Very little in the way of severe weather was observed with this initial round of storms. A warm front then lifted across the region through the day, bringing a very moist and unstable airmass to the area. Storms begin firing during the afternoon hours ahead of a cold front bringing all modes of severe weather during the late afternoon and evening hours before transitioning to more of a low end hail and damaging wind threat through the overnight hours and into the morning of the 9th. Numerous reports of hail and wind damage were received along with some minor flash flooding due to heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of storms and heavy rainfall led to minor flash flooding with water over roads in Iron River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of tropical storm Cristobal moved north-northeast across eastern Iowa, and into southwest and central Wisconsin during the evening of Tuesday, June 9th. The circulation associated with the system brought record high precipitable water values across parts of the Upper Midwest. These high values combined with a nearly stationary area of showers and embedded thunderstorms that moved from southeast Minnesota, northeast into west central Wisconsin, produced high rainfall rates and amounts. By the time the system moved out of the area, two to four inches of rainfall occurred in less than 3-6 hours. These high rainfall values caused several roads to become impassable in portions of Eau Claire and Chippewa Counties of west central Wisconsin. One road near Cadott was washed out, and another road was closed for several hours near Allen, or south of Eau Claire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads became impassable during this flash flooding event, including County Road D by Marshall Road where the Beaver Creek went out of its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Portions of far northwestern Wisconsin had seen rainfall deficits in excess of 5 inches for the year and by July, areas of Severe Drought (D2) were observed across parts of the area. A wet middle part of July led to improving conditions that brought all areas into the Moderate Drought (D1) category by the end of the month, however, rainfall deficits persist and further dry conditions may reverse the improving trend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened at the beginning of July as rainfall deficits for the year approached 5 to 7 inches. A few rainfall events during the middle of the month resulted in total rainfall for the month in the 3 to 5.50 inch range, with Duluth/Superior seeing totals about 1.50 inches above normal. This was enough to lift the area out of the Severe Drought category (D2), especially with the rainfall received on July 18 and 21, when around 1 to 2 inches total fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of thunderstorms ahead of a cold front led to rainfall amounts of 2 to 5 inches in a short amount of time across parts of Price and Sawyer Counties. Numerous roads and county highways had water over them or were washed out. A few communities in Price County issued disaster declarations due to washed out roads due to culvert failures. In one community alone damage to roads was estimated at $25,000 to $30,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some streets were flooded and closed in the town of Phillips.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms rolled across western Wisconsin during the early morning of July 19th. These storms produced an EF-1 tornado southwest of Osseo (Trempealeau County) that destroyed some farm outbuildings and snapped or uprooted numerous trees. The storms also blew down trees near Friendship (Adams County) and south of Brooks (Adams County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted just north of Osseo.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The water level on Musser Lake rose to one foot above normal levels and triggered the opening of all but one gate on the Musser Dam. The increased outflow then rose over the Musser Drive bridge just downstream of the day for a period of time resulting in the road being closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and a cold front moved across the Northland on the afternoon and evening of the 18th. Ahead of the cold front, dew points surged into the 70s and, coupled with the cold front, led to the development of a few supercell thunderstorms that moved across the region. The storms developed over north-central Minnesota during the afternoon and pushed eastward through northwest Wisconsin during the evening hours producing damaging winds and large hail as they pushed across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of approximately one half mile long of trees were blown down along Blueberry Road between Frog Bay Road and Emil Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of tropical storm Cristobal moved north-northeast across eastern Iowa, and into southwest and central Wisconsin during the evening of Tuesday, June 9th. The circulation associated with the system brought record high precipitable water values across parts of the Upper Midwest. These high values combined with a nearly stationary area of showers and embedded thunderstorms that moved from southeast Minnesota, northeast into west central Wisconsin, produced high rainfall rates and amounts. By the time the system moved out of the area, two to four inches of rainfall occurred in less than 3-6 hours. These high rainfall values caused several roads to become impassable in portions of Eau Claire and Chippewa Counties of west central Wisconsin. One road near Cadott was washed out, and another road was closed for several hours near Allen, or south of Eau Claire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured rainfall report of 2.87 inches occurred over the past 12 hours near Augusta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Sunday, June 28th, a complex of thunderstorms that developed across Iowa previously, moved slowly northward across southern Minnesota and western Wisconsin before stalling Monday morning. During the onset of the heavier rainfall, a few storms caused wind damage near Tainter Lake and Colfax, but most of the damage was minor with a few large tree limbs and power lines blown down. ||Deep moisture convergence along the axis of thunderstorms, training along the periphery of the the numerous boundaries, combined with PWAT values of 1.7 to 1.9 inches which is over the 99th percentile value, caused extreme rainfall rates. ||Before the flash flooding event developed, there was an hour or two during Sunday evening that dropped locally one to two inches of rainfall in the areas that received more torrential rainfall in the early morning hours. ||There was one main band of excessive rainfall that occurred in west central Wisconsin during the early morning. This area was from Prescott, northeast toward River Falls and Baldwin. Based on the return period from precipitation amounts in a specific time frame, the values were a 100-200 year event. Flash flood guidance values were 1.5 to 2 times above normal.  ||As the runoff continued through the morning, the flash flood event become more areal flooding as the flood waters receded into creeks, streams, and eventually the mainstem rivers. Other areas that experienced flooding but not as significant include the New Richland Golf Course. The St. Croix County Sheriff Office documented the flood damage the next day from their drone. From the video, you could see numerous culverts and roads that were washed away including crop fields (near the creeks and streams) that were inundated nearly 24 hours after the initial rainfall started. ||Some of the higher rainfall totals around the area are as follows.||Emerald measured 9.84 inches.|Baldwin measured 8.72 inches. |River Falls measured 6.34 to 7.82 inches around the city. |Roberts measured 7.24 inches. |Menomonie measured 5.08 to 5.64 inches around the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trained spotters and the local fire department around Trainter Lake to Colfax reported a few large tree limbs blown down, along with some power lines.

A line of thunderstorms rolled across western Wisconsin during the early morning of July 19th. These storms produced an EF-1 tornado southwest of Osseo (Trempealeau County) that destroyed some farm outbuildings and snapped or uprooted numerous trees. The storms also blew down trees near Friendship (Adams County) and south of Brooks (Adams County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down southwest of Osseo and was on the ground for about 7 minutes as it traveled southeast. A couple of pole sheds and smaller farm buildings were destroyed. A feed mill business sustained roof damage and numerous trees were snapped or uprooted with some of the trees falling on homes. At least one home sustained significant damage when a downed tree went through the roof.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell near Onalaska.

Meteorologists were tracking a number of weak areas of rotation on radar as these storms moved through, and a Tornado Warning was issued for one such area of rotation at 1:10 AM CDT including Maple and Blueberry. At 1:43 AM CDT, meteorologists saw a dramatic increase in radar-detected rotation from this storm just south of Maple, WI, and based on this information issued an additional Tornado Warning using radar-confirmed tornado language to indicate the|confidence in a tornado occurring or imminent. Minutes later, a tornado developed north of Maple and Blueberry knocking down dozens of trees, including snapping some trees off near the base. Just as soon as it developed, the area of rotation weakened|considerably. Radar indicates this tornado may have begun a few minutes earlier but at this time there is not enough conclusive damage evidence that the tornado formed earlier than documented.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of tropical storm Cristobal moved north-northeast across eastern Iowa, and into southwest and central Wisconsin during the evening of Tuesday, June 9th. The circulation associated with the system brought record high precipitable water values across parts of the Upper Midwest. These high values combined with a nearly stationary area of showers and embedded thunderstorms that moved from southeast Minnesota, northeast into west central Wisconsin, produced high rainfall rates and amounts. By the time the system moved out of the area, two to four inches of rainfall occurred in less than 3-6 hours. These high rainfall values caused several roads to become impassable in portions of Eau Claire and Chippewa Counties of west central Wisconsin. One road near Cadott was washed out, and another road was closed for several hours near Allen, or south of Eau Claire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured rainfall report of 3.30 inches occurred over the past 12 hours east of Durand.

A slow moving cold front and upper low pressure area brought repeated thunderstorms to southern Milwaukee County during the evening. 3 to 7 inches of rainfall caused the flash flooding of streets and creeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to seven inches of rain from persistent thunderstorms resulted in the flash flooding of creeks, streets, parks, and residential yards in southern Milwaukee County. This included the communities of West Allis, Greenfield, Oak Creek, Franklin, and others in southern Milwaukee County.  Many streets were impassable and closed. Many vehicles were stranded in 2 to 4 feet of floodwaters. Portions of I-43 were closed due to flooding.  Many yards and basements were flooded especially when the floodwaters rose from the streets into the yards.  Oak Creek at South Milwaukee rapidly rose and nearly reached moderate flood stage.

Tornado was embedded within a line of storms that also had winds of 50-60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front followed by an approaching low pressure area and cold front led to 17 to 21 hours of strong to severe thunderstorms and heavy rainfall over portions of central and southern WI. 4 to 6 inches of rain fell in northern Columbia County in the late afternoon into the early evening of August 27th, with additional rain afterward. Considerable flash flooding occurred in Columbia County. In addition, several reports of tree damage via downburst or straight-line winds were received across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many roads impassible in Portage from flash flooding. Many vehicles stranded or flowing within the deep floodwaters with several water rescues executed.  Also, yards and basements flooded in Portage. Elsewhere in northern Columbia County, road and culvert washouts on highway P east of Pardeeville, and in the townships of Fort Winnebago and Lewiston. Rising waters on Swan Lake flooded out some homes on the northwest side of the lake. Portions of the Fox River flooded from northeast Columbia County to Pardeeville. The Fox River floodwaters flooded a portion of highway 44 northeast of Marcellon. The Pardeeville DPW sandbagged the sanitary sewer manholes to reduce the constant running of the lift stations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A young swimmer and the man who tried to save him perished off McKinley Beach at the Milwaukee lakeshore.  Breezy south winds resulted in moderate waves and dangerous underwater currents along the shore, and near piers and breakwalls.  Wave heights were estimated to be 2 to 4 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A young swimmer and the man who tried to save him died due to drowning at McKinley Beach at the Milwaukee lakeshore.  Breezy south winds resulted in moderate waves and dangerous underwater currents along the shore, and near piers and breakwalls.  Wave heights were estimated to be 2 to 4 feet at the time of the incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, late season cold front worked through the Northland on the evening of the 25th. Ample shear was in place along with cold air aloft to lead to the development of storms that were efficient hail producers across parts of northwest Wisconsin. One of the storms was able to tap into the strong shear and produce a brief, weak tornado that produced tree damage near Upson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief, weak tornado caused tree damage across mainly county-owned forested land southwest of Upson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level shortwave produced thunderstorms across north-central Wisconsin during the evening hours.  Three tornadoes and wind damage was reported as these thunderstorms tracked through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed at 9:12 PM CDT in a remote forested area in extreme northeast Forest County, east of the town of Alvin.  The tornado moved northeast to about 0.3 miles north of Ransdell Lake, then turned east, reaching maximum width before crossing Huff Creek Road.  The tornado produced a path of tree damage (DI 27,28, DOD 3,4) before entering Florence County at 9:18 PM CDT. Peak winds were estimated at 105 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level shortwave produced thunderstorms across north-central Wisconsin during the evening hours.  Three tornadoes and wind damage was reported as these thunderstorms tracked through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado that formed at 9:12 PM CDT in extreme northeast Forest County crossed the county line at 9:18 PM CDT.  The tornado continued east, producing a path of tree damage (DI 27,28, DOD 3,4) in a remote forested area in extreme northwest Florence County, before crossing the Brule River into Upper Michigan at 9:20 PM CDT. Peak winds were estimated at 105 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of storms moved across portions of western Wisconsin on August 10th. The first round occurred in the morning and produced a wind gust of 58 mph at the Black River Falls airport (Jackson County) and blew down some trees near New Miner (Juneau County) and Friendship (Adams County). The second round of storms occurred in the afternoon and produced a brief EF-0 tornado near Burton (Grant County). There were also several reports of wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph and numerous trees down across other parts of Grant County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather observing equipment at the Black River Falls airport measured a 58 mph wind gust.

Most of the hail was dime sized except for some quarter sized hail north of Sparta (Monroe County). A 58 mph wind gust also occurred at the La Crosse airport (La Crosse County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms with heavy rain occurred across portions of western Wisconsin during the early morning of August 28th. These storms were centered along and north of Interstate 94 from Monroe County southeast through Adams County. Rainfall amounts of 3 to 5 inches were common causing numerous roads to be closed with some washouts occurring. Northwest of Tunnel City (Monroe County) a milk truck hit a washed out portion of a road causing it to flip on its side. South of Oakdale (Monroe County) a mudslide removed a house from its foundation. Several homes in Camp Douglas (Juneau County) had water in the basements. The highest reported rainfall totals were 9.54 inches in New Lisbon (Juneau County) and 7.74 inches at Volk Field (Juneau County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused some flash flooding to occur across the southern portions of Adams County. Roads were washed out near Plainville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, late season cold front worked through the Northland on the evening of the 25th. Ample shear was in place along with cold air aloft to lead to the development of storms that were efficient hail producers across parts of northwest Wisconsin. One of the storms was able to tap into the strong shear and produce a brief, weak tornado that produced tree damage near Upson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level shortwave produced thunderstorms across north-central Wisconsin during the evening hours.  Three tornadoes and wind damage was reported as these thunderstorms tracked through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado produced a path of tree damage (DI 27,28, DOD 3,4) from the eastern shore of Star Lake to the Upper Bucktabon Springs State Natural Area. Peak winds were estimated around 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin during the early morning of August 31st. A lightning strike from one of these storms started a fire at a apartment building in La Crosse (La Crosse County). One unit in the apartment building sustained heavy fire and smoke damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike started a fire at an apartment building in La Crosse. One unit sustained heavy fire and smoke damage as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over west central Wisconsin, ahead of a cold front across eastern Minnesota early Thursday morning. The storms over eastern Minnesota developed an outflow boundary which led to additional thunderstorms development over western Wisconsin. Although a few large hail stones occurred and minor wind damage reported, most of the storms were sub-severe.

A cold front moved southeast across eastern Minnesota, and western Wisconsin during the late afternoon, and evening of Saturday, July 18th. Along this front, a line of severe thunderstorms develop which initially was producing large hail, but became tornadic during the evening north of Prescott, Wisconsin. This is the continuation of the Washington County tornado in east central Minnesota.

During the afternoon of Tuesday, July 21st, a few thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary over east-central Minnesota. Several spotters and storm chasers were following these storms as the storms moved into Wisconsin. ||The storm that moved into Polk County had a consistent funnel cloud associated with it with a brief tornado touchdown near Clear Lake and then again near Arland. Another storm developed across northern Dunn County and produced an EF0 tornado northeast of Wheeler. More storms developed over southeast Dunn and central Chippewa Counties to produce the majority of the damage. ||The worst damage occurred in the city of Cadott where numerous trees, power lines and a few structures were damaged as it moved through the town before dissipating.||The two tornadoes in Chippewa County had an average path length of 3 to 4 miles and an average width of 75 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser took video and pictures of the tornado, which lofted tree debris along a short path.
