EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of Tuesday, November, 10th, a cold front had moved across Minnesota and western Wisconsin. A 500 MB short wave that developed across the central Rockies was moving northeast across Nebraska with an intensifying 850 MB low developing across Iowa Tuesday morning. At the surface, low pressure was across northern Missouri. Initially, the 850 MB low moved northward across Iowa, and stronger warm air advection across eastern Minnesota and western Wisconsin kept the precipitation in the form of sleet, freezing rain, and some snow Tuesday morning. Farther to the west and deeper into the colder air, light snow fell but it was mostly flurries or occasionally 2-4SM in light snow.||A dry slot developed, and moved over eastern Minnesota, while the main storm system moved farther west. Once the upper low moved over southwest Minnesota, a broader area of precipitation started to develop over central Minnesota into west central Wisconsin, where the dendritic growth zone was maximized together with abundant moisture content and lift. Also during the later afternoon, the storm system became more organized as noted with a beautiful comma-shaped upper low moving over Minnesota. This was also where several snow bands started to develop, especially over central Minnesota into west central Wisconsin. where the combination of the intensifying short wave and upper-level jet occurred. At the surface, the low continued to strengthen and move northeast across eastern Iowa, and into northern Wisconsin by late evening. Over the rest of west-central Wisconsin, snowfall amounts of 1 inch or less occurred. Some freezing rain did occur Tuesday morning, and into the afternoon, but only isolated areas received enough to cause problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trained weather observers and area airports reported a mixture of sleet and light snow Tuesday afternoon. Heavier snowfall began during the late afternoon and evening before ending toward midnight. Snowfall amounts across the far northwest part of the county averaged 5 to 6 inches.

Several storms merged over Eau Claire County and developed a small bow segment that produced wind damage in Augusta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and large tree branches were blown down on the northwest part of Augusta. A few trees also landed on power lines elsewhere in Augusta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed across northwest Minnesota, propagated south-southeast became a well defined bow echo segment. This line segment, although not as intense moved across west central Wisconsin during the overnight hours, caused isolated wind damage around Cumberland and Augusta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were blown down near Cumberland.

This line segment, although not as intense moved across west central Wisconsin during the overnight hours, caused isolated wind damage around Cumberland and Augusta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple large tree branches, including one that landed on a power line, fell down near Augusta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved southeast across eastern Minnesota, and western Wisconsin during the late afternoon, and evening of Saturday, July 18th. Along this front, a line of severe thunderstorms develop which initially was producing large hail, but became tornadic during the evening north of Prescott, Wisconsin. This is the continuation of the Washington County tornado in east central Minnesota.
Thunderstorms developed along a warm front located across northern Wisconsin during the afternoon hours.  The strongest storms produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell in Merrill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure lifting northeastward brought a short but heavy period of snow to the region during the evening of the 10th before ending during the early morning hours. Snowfall reports of 5 to 7 inches were recorded in Douglas and Bayfield counties. Further east, warmer air led to freezing rain across portions of Ashland, Sawyer, Iron and Price counties with ice accumulations up to a quarter of an inch in spots leading to very slick roads and a few isolated power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 0.30 of ice accumulation near Mellen in about an hour leading to very slick roadways and scattered downed tree branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Tuesday, July 21st, a few thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary over east-central Minnesota. Several spotters and storm chasers were following these storms as the storms moved into Wisconsin. ||The storm that moved into Polk County had a consistent funnel cloud associated with it with a brief tornado touchdown near Clear Lake and then again near Arland. Another storm developed across northern Dunn County and produced an EF0 tornado northeast of Wheeler. More storms developed over southeast Dunn and central Chippewa Counties to produce the majority of the damage. ||The worst damage occurred in the city of Cadott where numerous trees, power lines and a few structures were damaged as it moved through the town before dissipating.||The two tornadoes in Chippewa County had an average path length of 3 to 4 miles and an average width of 75 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief funnel cloud was observed around Bear Lake in western Polk County. Video was inconclusive as to whether there was any ground circulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved across the area on December 23rd causing widespread non-thunderstorm wind gusts in the 35 to 50 mph range. The La Crosse airport did hit a peak gust of 58 mph and there were reports of sporadic damage around the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusted to 53 mph in the Lancaster area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure brought heavy snowfall and very strong winds to much of the Northland starting on Wednesday the 23rd and lingering into Thursday the 24th. Very strong frontogenesis occurred along a trowal axis that extended across much of northern Wisconsin west into Minnesota. Warm air advection caused temperatures across most of northwest Wisconsin to rise above freezing and produce mixed precipitation for several hours during the day. As the cold front gradually moved east, temperatures very quickly dropped and caused treacherous road conditions as mixed precipitation changed over to snow. Narrow bands of heavy snow set up over the Brainerd Lakes northeast towards the Iron Range during the early morning and early afternoon, and then translated east towards the Twin Ports, North Shore, and Interstate 35 corridor. The heavy snow bands coincided with the strong mid-level frontogenesis, which also produced very strong northwest winds that caused the widespread blizzard conditions. A few power outages were reported and widespread whiteout conditions made travel difficult if not impossible.||As the cold front swept rapidly eastward, bitterly cold air moved into the region and led to the development of a topographically-forced gravity wave during the night of the 23rd that dropped several more inches of snow from Two Harbors to Superior to Holyoke. The highest snowfall report was 13 inches of snow near Foxboro under this band. Further east, lake-effect snow continued into Thursday along the South Shore. Gile, Wisconsin, saw 18.5 inches of snow before lake-effect snow tapered off Thursday evening.

A surge of relatively mild and moist air aloft brought a swath of moderate to heavy snow to southern WI. The heaviest snow fell along and south of a line from Darlington to Jefferson to Port Washington, with nearly a foot of snow in portions of Green and Rock Counties. Snow covered and slippery roads led to some vehicle slide-offs and accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 4-8 inches with highest amounts in southern Dane County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure and an approaching cold front initiated a severe line of thunderstorms that caused swaths of straight-line wind damage across southeast WI. Numerous trees and tree limbs were knocked down along with some structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread tree damage and power outages in the Village of Fontana-On-Geneva-Lake.   Dozens of roads blocked by fallen trees.  At least 14 homes sustained structural damage due to fallen trees. A roof blown off a home. A green house destroyed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tall tree snapped off and landed onto the roof of a home in Sharon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of relatively mild and moist air aloft brought accumulating light snow during the morning rush hour. Some vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred across portions of southern WI. This included one fatality in Jefferson Co. when a vehicle lost control on a slippery highway and hit an oncoming vehicle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to three inches of snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Sunday, June 28th, a complex of thunderstorms that developed across Iowa previously, moved slowly northward across southern Minnesota and western Wisconsin before stalling Monday morning. During the onset of the heavier rainfall, a few storms caused wind damage near Tainter Lake and Colfax, but most of the damage was minor with a few large tree limbs and power lines blown down. ||Deep moisture convergence along the axis of thunderstorms, training along the periphery of the the numerous boundaries, combined with PWAT values of 1.7 to 1.9 inches which is over the 99th percentile value, caused extreme rainfall rates. ||Before the flash flooding event developed, there was an hour or two during Sunday evening that dropped locally one to two inches of rainfall in the areas that received more torrential rainfall in the early morning hours. ||There was one main band of excessive rainfall that occurred in west central Wisconsin during the early morning. This area was from Prescott, northeast toward River Falls and Baldwin. Based on the return period from precipitation amounts in a specific time frame, the values were a 100-200 year event. Flash flood guidance values were 1.5 to 2 times above normal.  ||As the runoff continued through the morning, the flash flood event become more areal flooding as the flood waters receded into creeks, streams, and eventually the mainstem rivers. Other areas that experienced flooding but not as significant include the New Richland Golf Course. The St. Croix County Sheriff Office documented the flood damage the next day from their drone. From the video, you could see numerous culverts and roads that were washed away including crop fields (near the creeks and streams) that were inundated nearly 24 hours after the initial rainfall started. ||Some of the higher rainfall totals around the area are as follows.||Emerald measured 9.84 inches.|Baldwin measured 8.72 inches. |River Falls measured 6.34 to 7.82 inches around the city. |Roberts measured 7.24 inches. |Menomonie measured 5.08 to 5.64 inches around the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Parts of St. Croix County received over 8 to 9 inches of rain causing flooding and washed out roads across the county. The worst areas that experienced significant flooding were the Kinnickinnic, Rush and Eau Galle Recreation River areas, and the towns of Hammond, Baldwin, Emerald, Glenwood City, and Cylon. ||Some of the specific roads that were closed or washed out during the height of the flooding were: 1000 Block of 190th Street in Baldwin, 2100 110th Avenue in Baldwin, Country Road TK, DD & 240th Street in Baldwin, Highway 12 & 220th Street in Baldwin Township, 2254 130th Avenue in Emerald, 2900 Block of 200th Avenue in Glenwood City, and West Blvd & Highway 65 in Roberts.||Unfortunately,  A man died after he lost control while driving on a water-covered road north of Baldwin near County Road E and Highway 63. At least five other motorists standing on the roofs of their cars were rescued from rushing water in that same location in the Baldwin area.||About $4 Million dollars in damages was due to 40 homes that sustained major damage and one that was destroyed when the foundation caved in. The other $4 Million dollars was due to bridge, highway and road damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking from the southern Great Plains to southern Lake Michigan spread a swath of 1-3 inches of snow across southern WI. Areas most impacted by the snow was along and north of a line from Darlington to Madison to Fond du Lac. Numerous vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred, including some road closures due to those incidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 2-3 inches.

South winds gusted to 51 knots (59 mph) during the afternoon and early evening hours on Washington Island ahead of an approaching cold front.  Intermittent power and internet outages were noted on Washington Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South winds gusted to 51 knots (59 mph) causing intermittent power and internet outages on Washington Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms rolled across western Wisconsin during the late afternoon and evening of June 2nd. Some of these storms produced very large hail ranging in size from 1 inch up to 2.5 inches in diameter. One of the hardest hit areas by the hail was Holmen (La Crosse County) where it was estimated that between 4,000 and 5,000 homes would need new roofs. Up to golf ball sized hail also fell near Pray (Jackson County) and near Dodge (Buffalo County). The storms also produced damaging winds with trees blown down across parts of Jackson, Monroe, Vernon, Adams and Juneau Counties. In Tomah (Monroe County), the roof canopy was blown off a grandstand at the county fairgrounds. The metal siding was ripped off a grain mill southeast of Ontario (Vernon County). More than 6,600 customers in the La Crosse area (La Crosse County) were without power after the storms moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 65 mph gust blew down trees and branches on the south side of Holmen. Wind blown hail also damaged siding, window screens, downspouts, and plants.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from Tropical Depression Cristobal fell across western Wisconsin on June 9th. This heavy rain resulted in some flash flooding across portions of Buffalo, Trempealeau and Taylor Counties. In Fountain City (Buffalo County), water entered into a home when the levels reached as high as a door. High water covered some city streets in Arcadia (Trempealeau County) and up to a foot of water covered roads in and near Gilman (Taylor County). The highest reported rainfall totals were 3.87 inches in Cochrane (Buffalo County) and 3.66 inches southeast of Mondovi (Buffalo County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest of Trempealeau, 3.20 inches of rain fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent surge of relatively moist and unstable air north of a stationary front brought heavy rain and flash flooding to portions of south central WI. 4-6 inches of rain fell. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of rain led to some minor road flooding between La Valle and Reedsburg. A section of Birchwood Rd. was washed out and closed on the west side of Lake Delton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early winter storm produced 6 to 9 inches across a portion of west-central Wisconsin during the afternoon and early evening of Tuesday, October 20th. A weak surface trough moved across the Upper Midwest with modest warm air advection ahead of it. Initially, precipitation was light but increased by the late morning and afternoon as both higher than average Precipitable Water values were ingested into the system and a dual upper jet core was maximized across Minnesota. This allowed for a few hours of higher snowfall rates and led to isolated 8-9 inch totals where banding of these higher snowfall rates were located.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across the county ranged from 4 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms rolled across western Wisconsin during the late afternoon and evening of June 2nd. Some of these storms produced very large hail ranging in size from 1 inch up to 2.5 inches in diameter. One of the hardest hit areas by the hail was Holmen (La Crosse County) where it was estimated that between 4,000 and 5,000 homes would need new roofs. Up to golf ball sized hail also fell near Pray (Jackson County) and near Dodge (Buffalo County). The storms also produced damaging winds with trees blown down across parts of Jackson, Monroe, Vernon, Adams and Juneau Counties. In Tomah (Monroe County), the roof canopy was blown off a grandstand at the county fairgrounds. The metal siding was ripped off a grain mill southeast of Ontario (Vernon County). More than 6,600 customers in the La Crosse area (La Crosse County) were without power after the storms moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were blown down in Hatfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms rolled across western Wisconsin during the late afternoon and evening of June 2nd. Some of these storms produced very large hail ranging in size from 1 inch up to 2.5 inches in diameter. One of the hardest hit areas by the hail was Holmen (La Crosse County) where it was estimated that between 4,000 and 5,000 homes would need new roofs. Up to golf ball sized hail also fell near Pray (Jackson County) and near Dodge (Buffalo County). The storms also produced damaging winds with trees blown down across parts of Jackson, Monroe, Vernon, Adams and Juneau Counties. In Tomah (Monroe County), the roof canopy was blown off a grandstand at the county fairgrounds. The metal siding was ripped off a grain mill southeast of Ontario (Vernon County). More than 6,600 customers in the La Crosse area (La Crosse County) were without power after the storms moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to golf ball sized hail fell near Pray.

One of the hardest hit areas by the hail was Holmen (La Crosse County) where it was estimated that between 4,000 and 5,000 homes would need new roofs. Up to golf ball sized hail also fell near Pray (Jackson County) and near Dodge (Buffalo County). The storms also produced damaging winds with trees blown down across parts of Jackson, Monroe, Vernon, Adams and Juneau Counties. In Tomah (Monroe County), the roof canopy was blown off a grandstand at the county fairgrounds. The metal siding was ripped off a grain mill southeast of Ontario (Vernon County). More than 6,600 customers in the La Crosse area (La Crosse County) were without power after the storms moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The metal siding was ripped off the side of a grain mill southeast of Ontario.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front advancing out of the northwest incited severe thunderstorm development in the afternoon across northwestern WI. A few of the storms produced hail up to the size of quarter to golf ball size before transitioning into wind producing storms further east that took down trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dozen ore more healthy trees were downed along Highway 13 between Griffith Road and Neuberger Road north of Butternut.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracking across Illinois, northern Indiana, and lower MI brought a swath of accumulating, wet snow to much of southern WI. Some areas received 6-10 inches. Far eastern WI, especially near Lake MI, experienced mostly rain. There were some vehicle slide-offs and accidents over the area including 16 accidents and 14 vehicle slide-offs in western Milwaukee County. There was one indirect death related to a car accident on a slippery highway in Dodge County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 3-5 inches with much less in the far northwest portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A western Wisconsin woman passed away in northern Adams County just before Christmas as a result of hypothermia. Local officials said the woman's car was found submerged up to the roof in a water filled ditch but she was able to escape from the car. However, with soaking wet clothes and air temperatures in the single digits above zero, hypothermia quickly set in and her body was found two days after the accident just a few hundred yards from the crash site.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A western Wisconsin woman died in northern Adams County as a result of hypothermia. Local officials found the woman's car submerged up to the roof in a water filled ditch. The woman escape from the car but with soaking wet clothes and temperatures in the single digits above zero, hypothermia quickly set in and her body was found two days after the accident just a few hundred yards from the car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved across the area on December 23rd causing widespread non-thunderstorm wind gusts in the 35 to 50 mph range. The La Crosse airport did hit a peak gust of 58 mph and there were reports of sporadic damage around the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 56 mph at the Boscobel Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough deepened as it pivoted across Minnesota late on the 29th into the 30th and its associated surface low brought widespread snowfall to the region. Despite the strong dry air that was originally in place, the column of air saturated quickly allowing more accumulating snow then originally expected. Overall, snow totals yielded 3-6 inches with locally higher amount upwards of 12 inches across the Bayfield Peninsula where lake enhancement occurred.

A surge of relatively mild and moist air aloft brought a swath of moderate to heavy snow to southern WI. The heaviest snow fell along and south of a line from Darlington to Jefferson to Port Washington, with nearly a foot of snow in portions of Green and Rock Counties. Snow covered and slippery roads led to some vehicle slide-offs and accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 7-11 inches with highest amounts in southern and eastern Green County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Wednesday, December 23rd, snow, mixed with rain, sleet, and freezing rain, was observed across much of Minnesota, and west-central Wisconsin as an intense weather system moved from west to east through the region. It produced several inches of snow with rates as high as 1-2 inches per hour at times during the height of the storm. |Temperatures plummeted with strong winds gusting as high as 30-45 mph out of the northwest behind the low pressure system. Temperatures reached as high as the mid-40s during the morning before bottoming out in the single digits to teens above zero by midnight. ||A robust mid to upper-level trough approaching the region continued to strengthen before closing off, with a surface response in the form of a deepening low pressure system. This combined with incredible mid-level lapse rates produced significant lift and widespread precipitation, with an intense pressure gradient resulting in strong winds as the system moved eastwards. ||Winds intensified alongside bands of heavy snow during the late afternoon, which resulted in widespread snow. Visibilities dropped below 1 mile across the region and remained low for several hours. Snowfall diminished during the late evening and into early Thursday morning but winds remained strong enough to support blowing snow conditions into the mid-morning on Thursday in some spots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mainly rain, mixed with sleet fell Wednesday morning and early afternoon across the county. As much colder air arrived during the afternoon, temperatures dropped quickly which changed a mixture of precipitation over to snow. The snow became heavy by the late afternoon. By early evening, blowing snow developed and continued through the evening. Wind gusts of 25 to 35 mph were frequently achieved during the height of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Onshore flow and moist air from Lake MI flowing over sub-freezing roads resulted in frost formation on the Milwaukee freeway system. Numerous vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred during the morning. Small portions of all interstates in Milwaukee County were closed for a period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous vehicle slide-offs and accidents on the Milwaukee County freeway system due to frost formation on sub-freezing roads during the morning. Small portions of I-794, |I-43, I-94, I-41, and I-894 were all closed for a period of time.
